title: Sibling Rivalry as Adult Resentment
dek: *The insult happened thirty years ago but your jaw still locks when they enter the room. Childhood allegiances petrify into adult resentment that lives not in your memory but in your shoulders, in the heat that rises when you’re relegated again to the role you didn’t choose. This is not about forgiveness. It’s about what the body remembers and what it demands you finally acknowledge.*
bodyMarkdown:
She is forty-two years old, pouring wine into a glass that is already half full, and her older brother says, “Still no self-control, huh?” The kitchen is warm from the oven, their parents’ house unchanged, the same rooster clock on the wall. She feels her throat close, not with a lump—more like a drawstring pulled tight. She laughs, a dry bark. “Some things never change,” she says, and for the rest of the night her hands are cold, even after she wraps them around the hot mug of tea her mother hands her later. She watches him tell a story about his promotion and she is twelve again, locked out of the bathroom, missing the school bus, screamed at for borrowing a sweater. The rage is not a memory. It is a current.

## The Argument at the Kitchen Table Was Never Just About the Dishes

Adult sibling resentment performs a trick of time. The fight about who forgot to call Dad on his birthday is freighted with thirty years of unfairness. You’re not reacting to the missed call. You’re reacting to the pattern: that you carry the emotional labor while your sibling glides through with the lightness of someone who has never been expected to hold the center. Karla McLaren, in her work on the language of emotions, offers a precise lens: anger is not the problem; anger is the boundary-setter that arises when something you value is threatened. What you value, in this case, is not just the birthday call. It is justice, reciprocity, the hope that someone else might finally notice you’ve been doing all the heavy lifting. The anger you feel is a messenger, not a malfunction.

The body remembers before the mind can narrate. At that kitchen table, you might notice your stomach tighten, a band of pressure across your ribs. This is your diaphragm bracing, a somatic echo of every time you had to swallow a retort because your sibling was the favorite, the fragile one, the one whose needs came first. Peter Levine, the pioneer of somatic experiencing, describes how trauma—and ongoing relational stress qualifies—gets locked into the body as incomplete motor impulses. You wanted to scream or walk out or slam the door, but you didn’t. That undischarged action lives on as chronic tension, a readiness that never fully deactivates. The argument about the dishes is an argument with the ghost of a child who was not protected.

## Your Childhood Bedroom Still Knows

Go back to that room, if it still exists, or just sit with the memory of it. Notice the slant of light through half-closed blinds. The poster you put up to prove you were a different person. The sound of a sibling on the other side of the wall, breathing or laugh-crying or talking on the phone in a voice you were never allowed to use. The body keeps the score in subtle ways: the hip you shift to one side because there was never enough space for you, the slight collapse of your chest when you anticipate being interrupted. Soraya Chemaly, in *Rage Becomes Her*, argues that unexpressed anger—particularly in women—becomes a physical hazard, linked to autoimmune disorders, chronic pain, and depression. She writes, “Anger is not what makes us sick. Silencing it is.” If you were the one who learned to smooth things over, to be the peacemaker, your body may still be contorting itself around a silence that was never truly yours.

And here is a counterintuitive claim: the resentment you carry might actually be a form of loyalty. To let go of the anger feels like betraying the child who was wronged, as if your adult peace would invalidate the injustice. You stay clenched not because you enjoy it but because the tension is the last living proof that something happened. This is the body’s strange allegiance to history, a binding spell that keeps you in the same room with the same person, frozen in time. Karla McLaren might say that this stuckness is anger with nowhere to go, a boundary that was never honourably established. The work is not to stop being angry. It is to let anger complete its rightful action: to name the wound, to protect what matters, to let the body finally exhale.

## When Your Sister Says “You’re Too Sensitive” and Your Nervous System Agrees

The language families use to dismiss anger is a catalog of diminishment: “You always overreact,” “Can’t you take a joke?” “That’s just how they are.” These phrases are not neutral. They are instructions for your nervous system to override its own signals. You learn to doubt the heat in your chest, the prickle at the back of your neck, and in doing so, you sever yourself from your most fundamental orienting system. The polyvagal theory, which underlies much of Peter Levine’s work, shows that the body continuously scans for safety. When your sibling’s tone drops into that familiar register of contempt or condescension, your heart rate variability shifts, your stomach knots—and then you tell yourself it’s nothing. But it is not nothing. It is information. The trick is that as children, we needed these relationships to survive, so our bodies learned to suppress the fight-or-flight response. As adults, the same suppression shows up as exhaustion, brain fog, or a persistent ache in the jaw from clenching overnight.

One woman I know describes the sensation of a phone call with her younger brother as “a slow freeze starting in my ankles and traveling up.” She can finish the call sound calm and reasonable, but afterward she needs an hour to lie down, her limbs heavy. The body has been holding a freeze response that makes perfect evolutionary sense: you cannot run from a sibling you live with, you cannot fight a caretaker’s favorite without risking attachment. So you immobilize a part of yourself. Resentment, then, is the thaw. It is the nervous system finally daring to say, “That was not okay.” The problem is that the thaw usually happens alone in your car or in the middle of the night, not in the moment when it could actually change the dynamic.

A little practice: next time you anticipate contact with this sibling, give yourself four minutes. Sit with your feet flat on the floor, somewhere quiet. Place one hand on your lower belly and one on your chest. Very gently, without changing your breath, just notice the rhythm of it. Ask yourself: “What is my body wanting to do right now?” Maybe it wants to push something away, maybe it wants to curl inward, maybe it wants to stand up. Let the impulse exist as a subtle movement—a micro-shift of weight, a slight tensing of a muscle. This is what Levine calls “pendulation,” dipping into the body’s action tendency without being overwhelmed by it. You’re not fixing anything. You’re letting the body finish a sentence it started decades ago. After four minutes, you might notice the tension has shifted, not because you made it go away but because you finally listened.

## The Somatic Trance of Role and Re-enactment

Siblings are assigned roles like parts in a dysfunctional play: the responsible one, the needy one, the peacemaker, the troublemaker. Even when the parents are gone, the play continues. You walk into a room and pick up your script. Your brother hands you yours with a single raised eyebrow. These roles are not just mental constructs; they are embodied postures. The responsible one holds their shoulders back, chin up, bracing for the next emergency. The needy one slumps, speech softer, always a little wanting. Peter Levine points out that our sense of self is grounded in these bodily shapes. Changing the script is not just a matter of “setting boundaries” cognitively. It requires a physical re-patterning. You might need to literally learn to slacken your jaw when you speak to them, or to feel your full weight on the soles of your feet instead of hovering as if you’re about to jump to help.

Niche researcher Soraya Chemaly underscores that anger is often the only emotion that can blow apart these entrenched roles. When a woman finally gets furious at her brother for his casual dismissal of her career, she is not being “crazy.” She is destabilizing a dynamic that required her to be small. The fury is proportional, even if the trigger seems trivial—a comment about her messy house, a joke about her driving. That fury is the compressed energy of every Thanksgiving she bit her tongue. The body unleashes it in a flood because it can no longer afford the cost of containment. The challenge is to ride that fury without becoming its casualty. This is where McLaren’s framework becomes practical: anger asks you to set a boundary, not to burn the village down. The question becomes: what specific boundary, made with your whole body, can you set now? Maybe it’s not answering the phone after 7 p.m. Maybe it’s saying, “I won’t be spoken to that way,” and then physically leaving the room, your legs carrying you out even as your voice trembles.

[When the Body Says No — Gabor Maté](https://www.amazon.com/dp/1583949933?tag=spankyspinola-20) explores the intimate connection between suppressed emotion and disease. While Maté isn’t the primary researcher I’m referencing here, his clinical stories resonate with the sibling-resistance pattern: people who learned to say yes when their bodies screamed no often develop chronic conditions. The book sits on my shelf as a reminder that resentment is not a moral failing—it’s a physiological alarm. If you’re waking at 3 a.m. with your heart pounding after a weekend with family, your body is reading the data more honestly than your conforming mind.

## You Cannot Heal a Dynamic They Refuse to See

There is a grief that sits beneath adult sibling resentment, and it rarely gets acknowledged. You are grieving the sibling you hoped they would become, or the relationship you were promised in the storybooks of childhood. You’re grieving that after all this time, they still don’t see you. That grief can feel like a hollow ache in the center of the chest, a sensation of sinking inward. It is not the sharp stab of anger but the dull thud of disappointment that has become part of your baseline state. Karla McLaren distinguishes between the soft sadness of letting go and the heavy sadness of loss. This is the heavy one, the sadness of realizing that the person you’re heartbroken over may never say, “I’m sorry. I get it now.” Your body metabolizes this as a kind of ongoing stress, a low-grade inflammation that can show up as sleep disruption, digestive upset, or that feeling of never quite being well.

Sometimes the only sane response is distance, and distance is also a physical practice. It involves noticing the signals your body gives you when you’ve had too much: a crawling sensation on your skin, a sudden need to yawn, a feeling of your battery dropping from 40% to 5% in five minutes. The body can become a reliable gauge for time limits and topic boundaries. If you’ve tried everything and the resentment surges back with contact, consider that your anger is not overreacting—it’s telling you the ecosystem is still toxic. Soraya Chemaly writes that anger is a “vital political emotion,” and in the small politics of family, it exposes power imbalances you might prefer to ignore. When a sibling has never acknowledged their advantage or harm, your anger is a reasonable response to ongoing injustice, not a grudge you’re holding out of spite.

## The Practice of Letting the Body Speak While You Stay Still

Try this the next time you’re alone after an interaction that left you seething. Lie on your back on the floor, knees bent or legs up on a chair, so your spine is supported and your belly can soften. Close your eyes. Recall a specific moment from the exchange—not the whole story, just a five-second slice that holds the most charge. Let the scene play silently. Now, notice what happens in your body. You might feel a flush, a trembling in your thighs, a pressure behind your eyes. Give the sensation your full attention without trying to change it. If an impulse to move arises—a leg wants to kick, a hand wants to push—let that movement happen slowly, in a small dose. This is a form of Peter Levine’s “somatic experiencing,” allowing the trapped fight-or-flight energy to discharge. You’re not rehashing the story; you’re completing the biological survival response that got frozen. After a few minutes, let your body shift into whatever position feels restful. Notice the aftermath. A sigh, a yawn, a deeper breath. This is the nervous system settling, and it teaches you a different ending than the one you’ve been living.

If sleep is a casualty—if you lie awake rehearsing the things you should have said, the perfect comeback that came two hours too late—your adrenals are likely on high alert. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20), which combines ashwagandha, passionflower, and other calming botanicals, can help downshift that wired-but-tired state so your body gets the signal that it’s safe to rest. It’s not a sedative that knocks you out; it’s a support for the switch from sympathetic overdrive to parasympathetic repair, which is exactly what your system needs after decades of hypervigilance around family.

## FAQ: What You’re Actually Asking

**Q: Is it normal to resent my sibling even though we’re adults and the childhood stuff should be behind us?**
Normal is a useless word here. The body doesn’t have a statute of limitations. What you feel is a nervous system that learned a pattern of threat and is still reacting to a cue—the sibling’s voice, the family home, the holiday table—as if the original danger is present. Resentment is not a personality defect; it’s the accumulation of unacknowledged breaches. When someone has never been held accountable, the hurt doesn’t vanish. It compounds. You’re not immature. You’re a mammal with a memory.

**Q: How do I stop feeling this anger? It’s eating me alive.**
You stop trying to silence it and start listening. Anger is a messenger that you’ve endured an injustice. Karla McLaren’s framework suggests asking anger: “What must be protected?” and “What boundary needs to be set?” That might mean you dramatically reduce contact, you stop being the family event organizer, or you speak a truth that terrifies you. The anger itself isn’t the enemy—the lack of honorable action is what chews on you. Once you act on the message, the anger can recede, not because you “forgave” but because you stood up for the self you were taught to abandon.

**Q: Can a sibling relationship heal if only one person wants to do the work?**
It can heal inside you, even if the relationship doesn’t change externally. That’s a hard truth. Healing might look like reaching a place where contact no longer dysregulates you, not because they are different but because your body has learned to stay anchored. That’s the somatic work—titrating exposure while building your capacity to remain present. It may also mean grieving the loss of what you hoped for. A reconciled relationship is possible only when both siblings can see the dynamic and are willing to unhook from old roles. You can’t force that. What you can do is stop sacrificing your health to maintain a fiction. That’s your body’s demand, and it’s not asking for permission anymore.

[Gaia Herbs Ashwagandha Root (60 capsules)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0719D8GCB?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a single-herb support for the chronically stressed body. Ashwagandha, an adaptogen, helps regulate cortisol and can ease that constant low-level anxiety that hums beneath sibling interactions. It’s not a fix for the relational wound, but it can give your body a little more capacity to stay regulated when you’re around the person who spikes your system—so you can choose your response instead of being hijacked by a chemical cascade.

## The Body Will Not Be Gaslit

Your resentment is not a story you tell yourself to feel victimized. It is a real-time physiological event. When your sister’s name lights up your phone and your stomach drops, that is your gut’s enteric nervous system casting a vote of no confidence in the conversation to come. When your brother says “You’re so rigid” and your shoulders hike toward your ears, your body is mounting a defense against an attack that already landed. These are not cognitive distortions—they are accurate reads of a relational pattern that has not shifted. The work, then, is to stop talking yourself out of what your body knows. The body remembers, and until the memory is honoured, it will continue to speak in symptoms, in fatigue, in a jaw that aches in the morning from grinding through one more family dinner.

And there’s a peculiar freedom in this. Once you stop trying to fix your anger with forced forgiveness or positive affirmations, you can actually feel the anger as a clear, sharp sensation—maybe a heat in the palms, a ringing in the ears, a sudden alertness. This is activation without story. It’s energetic. Levine calls it the “life force” that gets bound up in trauma but that can be reclaimed. When you let the sensation exist without layering the narrative of “I’m a bad sibling for feeling this,” the anger shifts from chronic resentment into something more useful: a clear knowing that you deserve better. From that knowing, you can act. Not from the old script of pleasing or fighting, but from a body that finally trusts its own data.

## The Unsupervised Inheritance

Perhaps the most unsentimental truth is this: your sibling did not create the whole wound. They were a vessel. The actual architects were the parents who pitted you against each other, the culture that rewarded compliance, the family system that needed someone to be the scapegoat and someone to be the golden child. The resentment you feel toward your sibling may be displaced anger that would be too dangerous to aim at the original source. Your brother didn’t make himself the favorite, but he benefited from it and continues to act as if that’s just the natural order. Your sister didn’t invent the narrative that you’re “too emotional,” but she wielded it to get her way and still smirks when you try to speak seriously. The body’s confusion is that it has tangled the pain of parental neglect with the sibling’s face, and now any interaction with them fires off the whole trauma network. Unthreading this takes time, but it begins with the simple permission to feel exactly what you feel without shaming yourself.

For more on reading your body’s cues in tough relationships, you might find the essays at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) a useful companion—real explorations of anger and embodiment without the bypassing. The body never lies, though it often whispers in symptoms we dismiss as incidental. Your resentment is a directional signal, a homing beacon pointing to what needs to change. Maybe that change is a conversation held with trembling hands. Maybe it’s a silent goodbye. Whatever it is, it starts with the body’s blunt report: this hurts, and I’m not going to pretend it doesn’t anymore.

inlineProducts.title: When the Body Says No — Gabor Maté
